notes.title: Some Basic Concepts of Chemistry — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
The foundation of chemistry: mole concept, stoichiometry and concentration terms.

## 1. Laws of Chemical Combination
- Law of conservation of mass, constant proportions, multiple proportions, Gay-Lussac's law of gaseous volumes, Avogadro's law.

## 2. Mole Concept
- 1 mole = 6.022×10²³ particles (Avogadro number, Nₐ).
- Moles = given mass/molar mass = number of particles/Nₐ = volume(at STP)/22.4 L.
- Molar mass (g/mol) numerically equals average atomic/molecular mass (u).

## 3. Stoichiometry
- Balanced equations give mole ratios. Identify the **limiting reagent** (completely consumed) — it decides the product amount.
- Percentage yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100.

## 4. Concentration Terms
- **Molarity** M = moles solute/litre solution (temperature dependent).
- **Molality** m = moles solute/kg solvent (temperature independent).
- **Mole fraction**, mass %, ppm.

## 5. Empirical & Molecular Formula
- Empirical = simplest whole-number ratio; Molecular = n × empirical (n = molar mass/empirical mass).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Molarity changes with temperature, molality does not.
- Always identify the limiting reagent before calculating product.
- 22.4 L per mole is valid only for gases at STP.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Moles = mass/molar mass = N/Nₐ.
- Limiting reagent controls yield.
- Molality is temperature independent.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Most numericals reduce to 'convert everything to moles first' — do that and the ratios fall out.

notes.title: Structure of Atom — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Atomic models, quantum numbers and electronic configuration.

## 1. Subatomic Particles & Models
- Electron (Thomson), proton, neutron. Rutherford (nuclear), Bohr (quantised orbits for H).
- Bohr: Eₙ = −13.6/n² eV; rₙ = 0.529 n² Å (for hydrogen).

## 2. Dual Nature & Uncertainty
- de Broglie: λ = h/mv. Heisenberg uncertainty: Δx·Δp ≥ h/4π.

## 3. Quantum Numbers
- **Principal (n):** shell/energy. **Azimuthal (l):** subshell/shape (0–(n−1): s,p,d,f).
- **Magnetic (mₗ):** orientation (−l to +l). **Spin (mₛ):** ±½.

## 4. Filling Rules
- **Aufbau** (increasing energy, n+l rule), **Pauli exclusion** (no two electrons same 4 quantum numbers), **Hund's rule** (singly fill degenerate orbitals first).
- Stability of half-filled and fully-filled subshells (e.g. Cr: 3d⁵4s¹, Cu: 3d¹⁰4s¹).

## 5. Orbitals
- s spherical, p dumb-bell. Node count: radial = n−l−1, angular = l, total = n−1.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- 4s fills before 3d (lower n+l), but 3d is removed first on ionisation.
- Cr and Cu have anomalous configurations for extra stability.
- Number of orbitals in a subshell = 2l+1; electrons = 2(2l+1).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Eₙ = −13.6/n² eV (H).
- Quantum numbers n, l, mₗ, mₛ.
- Aufbau, Pauli, Hund.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use the (n+l) rule to order subshell energies; for ties, lower n fills first.

notes.title: Classification of Elements & Periodicity — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
The modern periodic table and trends in properties.

## 1. Modern Periodic Law
- Properties are a periodic function of ATOMIC NUMBER (not mass).
- 7 periods, 18 groups; s, p, d, f blocks.

## 2. Atomic & Ionic Radius
- Decreases across a period (increasing nuclear charge), increases down a group.
- Cations smaller than parent atom; anions larger.
- Isoelectronic species: more protons → smaller radius.

## 3. Ionisation Enthalpy
- Energy to remove an electron. Increases across period, decreases down group.
- Exceptions: Be>B, N>O (stable half-filled/configuration effects).

## 4. Electron Gain Enthalpy & Electronegativity
- Electron gain enthalpy most negative for halogens; Cl > F (small size of F).
- Electronegativity: F is highest; increases across, decreases down.

## 5. Other Trends
- Metallic character decreases across, increases down. Non-metallic opposite.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- IE: N > O and Be > B due to electronic configuration stability.
- Cl has more negative electron gain enthalpy than F.
- Atomic radius increases down, decreases across.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Periodic law based on atomic number.
- IE, electronegativity increase across; radius decreases across.
- F most electronegative; Cl most negative ΔH_eg.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the standard exceptions (Be/B, N/O, F/Cl) — that's where MCQs trap students.

notes.title: Chemical Bonding & Molecular Structure — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
How atoms bond: ionic, covalent, VSEPR, hybridisation and molecular orbital theory.

## 1. Types of Bonds
- **Ionic** (electron transfer), **Covalent** (sharing), **Coordinate** (one atom donates both electrons).
- **Lattice enthalpy** governs ionic bond strength (Born–Haber cycle).

## 2. VSEPR Theory
- Electron pairs arrange to minimise repulsion: lp–lp > lp–bp > bp–bp.
- Shapes: linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, octahedral. Lone pairs distort angles (e.g. H₂O bent 104.5°, NH₃ pyramidal 107°).

## 3. Hybridisation
- sp (linear, 180°), sp² (trigonal, 120°), sp³ (tetrahedral, 109.5°), sp³d, sp³d².

## 4. Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT)
- Bonding & antibonding MOs. **Bond order** = ½(Nb − Na).
- Predicts magnetism: O₂ is paramagnetic (2 unpaired electrons) — VBT fails here.

## 5. Polarity & Other Concepts
- Dipole moment μ = q×d; CO₂ nonpolar (linear, cancels), H₂O polar.
- **Hydrogen bonding** (HF, H₂O, NH₃) raises boiling points.
- Fajan's rules for covalent character in ionic bonds.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- More lone pairs → smaller bond angle.
- Bond order ∝ stability; bond order 0 means molecule doesn't exist.
- O₂ paramagnetism is explained only by MOT.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- VSEPR predicts shape; lone pairs distort angles.
- Bond order = ½(Nb−Na).
- H-bonding raises boiling point.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For shape questions, count bond pairs + lone pairs around the central atom, then apply VSEPR.

notes.title: Chemical Thermodynamics — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Energy changes in chemical reactions: enthalpy, entropy and Gibbs energy.

## 1. System & First Law
- ΔU = q + w (chemistry sign convention: w = −PΔV for expansion).
- State functions: U, H, S, G (path independent); q and w are path functions.

## 2. Enthalpy
- H = U + PV; ΔH = ΔU + Δ(PV). At constant P, q_p = ΔH.
- Exothermic ΔH < 0, endothermic ΔH > 0.
- **Hess's law:** total enthalpy change is independent of path (add reactions).

## 3. Types of Enthalpy
- Formation, combustion, neutralisation (strong acid+base ≈ −57.1 kJ/mol), bond enthalpy, hydration, sublimation.

## 4. Entropy & Spontaneity
- Entropy S = measure of disorder; ΔS > 0 favours spontaneity.
- **Gibbs energy** ΔG = ΔH − TΔS. Spontaneous if ΔG < 0; equilibrium if ΔG = 0.
- ΔG° = −RT ln K.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- A reaction can be endothermic yet spontaneous if TΔS outweighs ΔH.
- ΔU = q_v (constant volume); ΔH = q_p (constant pressure).
- ΔG < 0 means spontaneous (not 'fast').

## 📝 Quick Recap
- ΔU = q + w; ΔH = q_p.
- Hess's law: add enthalpies.
- ΔG = ΔH − TΔS (<0 spontaneous).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For spontaneity, always check the sign of ΔG = ΔH − TΔS, considering temperature's effect on the TΔS term.

notes.title: Equilibrium — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Physical & chemical equilibria, Le Chatelier's principle, and ionic equilibrium (acids/bases/buffers).

## 1. Chemical Equilibrium
- Dynamic: forward rate = backward rate. Equilibrium constant K_c (concentrations), K_p (partial pressures).
- K_p = K_c(RT)^{Δn}. Large K → products favoured.

## 2. Le Chatelier's Principle
- A system at equilibrium shifts to counteract a change in concentration, pressure or temperature.
- Increasing pressure shifts to fewer gas moles; increasing T favours the endothermic direction.
- A catalyst does NOT shift equilibrium (speeds both equally).

## 3. Ionic Equilibrium — Acids & Bases
- Arrhenius, Bronsted–Lowry (proton donor/acceptor), Lewis (electron pair acceptor/donor).
- Ionic product of water K_w = 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C; pH = −log[H⁺]; pH + pOH = 14.
- Strong acids/bases dissociate fully; weak ones partially (Ka, Kb, Ostwald's dilution law).

## 4. Buffers & Solubility
- **Buffer:** resists pH change. pH = pKa + log([salt]/[acid]) (Henderson equation).
- **Solubility product Ksp:** precipitation occurs when ionic product > Ksp. Common ion effect lowers solubility.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- A catalyst changes rate, NOT the position/value of equilibrium.
- K depends only on temperature.
- pH 7 is neutral only at 25°C.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- K_p = K_c(RT)^{Δn}.
- Le Chatelier: system opposes the change.
- pH = pKa + log([salt]/[acid]).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For 'shift of equilibrium' MCQs, apply Le Chatelier step-by-step for each change (conc, P, T).

notes.title: Redox Reactions — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Oxidation–reduction: oxidation numbers, balancing and redox titrations.

## 1. Concepts
- **Oxidation** = loss of electrons (increase in oxidation number); **Reduction** = gain of electrons.
- Oxidising agent gets reduced; reducing agent gets oxidised. (OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain.)

## 2. Oxidation Number Rules
- Free element = 0; O usually −2 (peroxide −1); H usually +1 (hydrides −1); sum = charge on species.

## 3. Balancing Redox
- **Oxidation number method** and **ion-electron (half-reaction) method** — balance atoms then charge using e⁻, H⁺/OH⁻ and H₂O.

## 4. Types & Applications
- Combination, decomposition, displacement, **disproportionation** (same element oxidised and reduced).
- Redox titrations (KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇). Electrochemical series predicts feasibility.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- The oxidising agent itself is REDUCED (and vice versa).
- In disproportionation, one element undergoes both oxidation and reduction.
- Balance charge as well as atoms.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Oxidation = loss of e⁻; reduction = gain.
- Oxidising agent is reduced.
- Use half-reaction method to balance.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Assign oxidation numbers first — the element whose number rises is oxidised, the one that falls is reduced.

notes.chapter: Organic Chemistry: Basic Principles and Techniques
notes.title: Organic Chemistry — Basic Principles — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Fundamentals of organic chemistry: nomenclature, isomerism, electronic effects and reaction intermediates.

## 1. Classification & IUPAC Nomenclature
- Identify the longest chain, functional group (suffix), substituents (prefix), lowest locants.
- Functional group priority order for naming.

## 2. Isomerism
- **Structural:** chain, position, functional, metamerism, tautomerism.
- **Stereoisomerism:** geometrical (cis/trans), optical (chirality, enantiomers).

## 3. Electronic Effects
- **Inductive (I):** permanent, through sigma bonds (−I withdraws, +I donates).
- **Resonance (M):** delocalisation of π electrons.
- **Hyperconjugation:** stabilises carbocations/alkenes (no-bond resonance).
- **Electromeric:** temporary, in presence of reagent.

## 4. Reaction Intermediates
- **Carbocation** (stability 3° > 2° > 1°), **carbanion** (reverse), **free radical**, **carbene**.

## 5. Reagents & Reactions
- **Electrophiles** (electron-loving) and **nucleophiles** (nucleus-loving).
- Bond fission: homolytic (radicals) and heterolytic (ions).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Carbocation stability: 3° > 2° > 1° (opposite for carbanions).
- Resonance requires conjugation/adjacent π or lone pair.
- More hyperconjugation (more α-H) → more stable.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- IUPAC: longest chain + functional group suffix.
- +I/−I, resonance, hyperconjugation.
- Carbocation 3°>2°>1°.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Stability of intermediates decides reaction pathways — master the order of carbocation/carbanion/radical stability.

notes.title: Hydrocarbons — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatic hydrocarbons with their key reactions.

## 1. Alkanes (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂)
- sp³, single bonds, saturated. Reactions: substitution (free radical halogenation), combustion, pyrolysis.
- Conformations of ethane (staggered more stable than eclipsed).

## 2. Alkenes (CₙH₂ₙ)
- sp², C=C double bond. **Markovnikov's rule** (H adds to C with more H); **anti-Markovnikov** (peroxide effect, HBr only).
- Reactions: addition of H₂, halogens, HX, water; ozonolysis; oxidation (Baeyer's reagent test).

## 3. Alkynes (CₙH₂ₙ₋₂)
- sp, C≡C triple bond. Terminal alkynes are acidic (react with Na, ammoniacal AgNO₃/Cu₂Cl₂).
- Addition reactions similar to alkenes.

## 4. Aromatic Hydrocarbons
- Benzene: planar, **Hückel's rule** (4n+2 π electrons), resonance-stabilised.
- **Electrophilic substitution:** nitration, sulphonation, halogenation, Friedel–Crafts alkylation/acylation.
- Directing effects: o/p-directors (−OH, −NH₂, alkyl) activate; m-directors (−NO₂, −COOH) deactivate.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Markovnikov reverses ONLY with HBr in presence of peroxide (Kharasch effect).
- Benzene undergoes substitution, not addition (to keep aromaticity).
- Terminal alkynes are acidic; internal ones are not.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Alkane substitution; alkene/alkyne addition.
- Markovnikov vs peroxide (anti-Markovnikov).
- Benzene: 4n+2 rule, electrophilic substitution.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For addition reactions, apply Markovnikov's rule unless HBr + peroxide is mentioned (then reverse).
